title: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation
text:
This document details the physical model and computational implementation of the Neutral Beam Injector (NBI) simulator for the TCV tokamak. The simulator calculates the 3D neutral density distribution of the beam, accounting for geometric focusing, Gaussian divergence, and plasma attenuation.
[cite_start]The propagation model is based on the theoretical framework described by Akhmetov et al., adapted for efficient numerical computation using MATLAB.
crumbs: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation

title: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation
text:
2.1 1. Beam Geometry and Focusing
The NBI system is modeled as a finite circular ion emitter of radius \(a\) (or source_radius). The emitter is curved to focus the beam at a specific focal length \(R\) (or s_focal).

Source: A flat or curved disk of radius \(a\) at \(z=0\) (or \(s=0\)).
[cite_start]Focus: The geometric focal point located at distance \(R\)[cite: 48].
[cite_start]Divergence: Each point on the emitter produces an “elementary beam” (beamlet) with a Gaussian angular divergence \(\theta_0\) (or alpha_u, alpha_v)[cite: 51].


2.1.1 The Elementary Beam
Consider a point on the emitter surface with coordinates \((\rho, \varphi)\). The axis of the elementary beam emitted from this point is directed toward the focal point. [cite_start]The current density contribution \(dj\) at an observation point \((r, z)\) depends on the angle \(\theta\) between the observation ray and the elementary beam axis[cite: 40].
The intensity follows a Gaussian distribution: \[dj \propto \exp\left( - \frac{\theta^2}{\theta_0^2} \right)\]
[cite_start]For small angles (\(\theta \ll 1\)), the angle \(\theta\) can be approximated as[cite: 79]: \[\theta^2 \approx \frac{r^2}{z^2} + \rho^2 \left( \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{R} \right)^2 - \frac{2\rho r}{z} \left( \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{R} \right) \cos\varphi\]
crumbs: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation

title: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation
text:
2.2 2. Total Current Density
[cite_start]The total current density at any point in the beam is found by integrating the elementary beamlets over the surface of the emitter[cite: 86].
\[j(r, z) = \int_0^a \rho d\rho \int_0^{2\pi} d\varphi \, C(z) \exp\left( - \frac{\theta^2(r,z,\rho,\varphi)}{\theta_0^2} \right)\]
where \(C(z)\) is a normalization factor ensuring conservation of current.
[cite_start]While Akhmetov et al. derive an analytical solution using Bessel functions for the specific case of a circular emitter[cite: 90], our code implements a numerical integration over the source radius. This provides greater flexibility for handling non-circular sources or complex divergence profiles in the future.
crumbs: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation

title: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation
text:
2.3 3. Beam Attenuation
As the neutral beam penetrates the plasma, it is attenuated by ionization and charge-exchange collisions. The local attenuation coefficient \(\alpha\) is defined as:
\[\alpha(s) = n_e(s) \cdot \sigma_{eff}(E, n_e, T_e)\]

\(n_e(s)\): Electron density profile along the beam path.
\(\sigma_{eff}\): Effective stopping cross-section (interpolated from ADAS data).

The beam intensity at distance \(s\) is reduced by the optical depth \(\tau\):
\[I(s) = I_0(s) \cdot \exp\left( - \int_0^s \alpha(s') ds' \right)\]
The simulator also implements a “hard stop” condition for the inner wall. [cite_start]If the beam crosses the major radius threshold \(R < (R_0 - a)\), the attenuation \(\alpha\) is set to infinity (simulating wall absorption)[cite: 36, 171].
crumbs: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation

title: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation
text:
3.1 1. Coordinate Systems
The code utilizes three distinct coordinate systems to manage the 3D geometry efficiently:

Global Cartesian \((X, Y, Z)\): The standard tokamak laboratory frame. Used for the final voxel grid and plotting.
Beam Coordinates \((s^*, u, v)\): A local orthonormal basis attached to the beam.

\(s^*\): Coordinate along the beam centerline.
\(u, v\): Transverse coordinates perpendicular to the beam.

Integration Grid \((s_{grid}, t_{grid})\): A 2D planar grid aligned with the beam axis used to pre-calculate attenuation.
crumbs: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation

title: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation
text:
3.2 2. Key Algorithms

3.2.1 Optimization 1: Vectorized Attenuation (cumtrapz)
Instead of solving the attenuation integral \(\tau = \int \alpha ds\) individually for every voxel (which is computationally expensive), the code: 1. Defines a 2D slice plane along the beam centerline. 2. Calculates the plasma profiles (\(n_e, T_e\)) and cross-sections (\(\sigma\)) on this entire 2D plane at once. 3. Uses cumtrapz (cumulative trapezoidal integration) to integrate \(\tau\) along the \(s\)-direction in a single operation. 4. Maps this 2D \(\tau\) map back to the 3D grid using griddedInterpolant.


3.2.2 Optimization 2: Numerical Source Integration
The function beam_propagation computes the beam intensity \(j(r,z)\).

Inputs: Local coordinates \(U, V\), distance \(s\), and source radius \(r_s\).
Method: It discretizes the source radius \(x_0\) into \(N=40\) steps.
Vectorization: It pre-calculates geometric factors (like \(1/z\), \(1/R\)) outside the loop. Inside the loop, it accumulates the contribution of Gaussian beamlets emitted from concentric rings on the source.

This effectively solves Eq. (4) from the reference paper [cite: 86] numerically:
```matlab % Pseudocode representation of the integration loop in beam_propagation for ii = 1:N_steps x0 = source_ring_radius(ii); % Gaussian contribution from this ring (Vectorized over all pixels) contribution = exp(-x0^2 * C1 + x0 * C2) .* (erf(A) - erf(B)); Intensity = Intensity + contribution; end
crumbs: NBI Simulation: Mathematical Model & Code Implementation

title: Optical Spectrometer
text: An optical spectrometer (which can be either a spectrophotometer, a spectograph, or a spectroscope) is a device used to meausre properties of light over a specific portion of the electromagnetic spectrum. It is used in spectroscopy for producing spectral lines, in order to measure their wavelenghts and intensities1.

title: Optical Spectrometer
text: An optical spectrometer (which can be either a spectrophotometer, a spectograph, or a spectroscope) is a device used to meausre properties of light over a specific portion of the electromagnetic spectrum. It is used in spectroscopy for producing spectral lines, in order to measure their wavelenghts and intensities1.

title: Optical Spectrometer
text:
Working principle






Figure 1: Optical spectrometer scheme1.



As illustrated in Figure 1, a spectroscope generally uses a movable slit, some mirrors, and a diffraction grating. Through the slit it is possible to regulate the amount of light which is getting inside the device, which is then reflected by a first mirror on the diffraction grating. At this point the second mirror is adjusted such that only the radiation which the desired wavelength reaches the device.

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
These notes summarize Chapter 3 (“Formalism”) of Introduction to Quantum Mechanics (Griffiths & Schroeter, 3rd ed., 2018).
Big picture: quantum mechanics is (i) a state space (Hilbert space), (ii) observables as Hermitian operators, and (iii) a measurement postulate giving outcomes and probabilities. Once this is in place, tools like uncertainty relations, changes of basis, and Dirac notation become natural.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
These notes summarize Chapter 3 (“Formalism”) of Introduction to Quantum Mechanics (Griffiths & Schroeter, 3rd ed., 2018).
Big picture: quantum mechanics is (i) a state space (Hilbert space), (ii) observables as Hermitian operators, and (iii) a measurement postulate giving outcomes and probabilities. Once this is in place, tools like uncertainty relations, changes of basis, and Dirac notation become natural.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
2 Notation conventions

State of the system: a vector \(\psi\) in a complex Hilbert space.
In the position basis, the state has components \(\psi(x)\).
Inner product: \(\langle f, g \rangle = \int f^*(x)\, g(x)\, dx\) (for wavefunctions).
Expectation value: \(\langle A \rangle = \langle \psi, \hat A \psi \rangle\).
Standard deviation (uncertainty): \(\Delta A = \sqrt{\langle (\hat A-\langle A\rangle)^2\rangle}\).
Commutator: \([\hat A,\hat B] = \hat A\hat B - \hat B\hat A\).
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
3 1. Hilbert space

3.1 1.1 Vector space of states (why we restrict it)
Wavefunctions must be normalizable so that probabilities make sense: \[
\int |\psi(x)|^2\,dx = 1.
\] The natural setting is the space of square-integrable functions (physicists’ Hilbert space).


3.2 1.2 Inner product and norm
Define inner product (for functions): \[
\langle f,g\rangle = \int f^*(x)\,g(x)\,dx,
\] and norm: \[
\|f\| = \sqrt{\langle f,f\rangle}.
\] Key properties: linearity in the second slot, conjugate symmetry, and positivity.


3.3 1.3 Orthonormal sets and completeness

Normalized: \(\langle f,f\rangle=1\)
Orthogonal: \(\langle f,g\rangle=0\)
Orthonormal family \(\{f_n\}\): \(\langle f_m,f_n\rangle=\delta_{mn}\)

A set \(\{f_n\}\) is complete if any vector \(f\) can be expanded: \[
f = \sum_n c_n f_n.
\] If the basis is orthonormal, coefficients follow from “Fourier’s trick”: \[
c_n = \langle f_n, f\rangle.
\]


3.4 1.4 Schwarz inequality (workhorse inequality)
For any \(f,g\), \[
|\langle f,g\rangle|^2 \le \langle f,f\rangle\,\langle g,g\rangle.
\] This single inequality is the engine behind uncertainty relations.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
4 2. Observables and Hermitian operators

4.1 2.1 Observables as operators
An observable \(Q\) is represented by a linear operator \(\hat Q\) acting on states: \[
\psi \mapsto \hat Q\psi.
\]


4.2 2.2 Expectation values force Hermiticity
The expectation value of a measurable quantity must be real: \[
\langle Q\rangle = \langle \psi, \hat Q\psi\rangle \in \mathbb{R}.
\] This holds for all \(\psi\) only if \(\hat Q\) is Hermitian (self-adjoint in physicists’ usage).


4.3 2.3 Definition of Hermitian operator and adjoint
The adjoint \(\hat Q^\dagger\) is defined by: \[
\langle f,\hat Q g\rangle = \langle \hat Q^\dagger f, g\rangle \quad \forall f,g.
\] Hermitian means: \[
\hat Q^\dagger = \hat Q.
\]


4.4 2.4 Practical note: domains and boundary terms
For differential operators, Hermiticity often depends on:

boundary conditions (finite intervals),
decay at infinity (infinite line),
the domain of allowed functions.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
section: 5 3. Determinate states and the eigenvalue equation
text:
5 3. Determinate states and the eigenvalue equation

5.1 3.1 “Determinate state” = zero spread
A state is determinate for observable \(Q\) if every measurement returns the same value \(q\). Then: \[
\Delta Q = 0.
\] Using \(\Delta Q^2 = \langle (\hat Q-\langle Q\rangle)^2\rangle\), this implies: \[
(\hat Q - q)\psi = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \hat Q\psi = q\psi.
\]


5.2 3.2 Eigenvalues, spectrum, degeneracy

\(q\) is an eigenvalue, \(\psi\) an eigenstate/eigenfunction.
The set of all eigenvalues is the spectrum.
If multiple linearly independent eigenstates share the same eigenvalue, it’s degenerate.

Physical meaning: If the system is in an eigenstate of \(\hat Q\), then measuring \(Q\) is certain to yield the associated eigenvalue.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
6 4. Eigenfunctions of a Hermitian operator

6.1 4.1 Discrete spectra: the “matrix-like” case
For normalizable eigenfunctions:
Theorem 1 (Reality): eigenvalues of a Hermitian operator are real.
Theorem 2 (Orthogonality): eigenfunctions with distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.
Degenerate subspaces can be orthonormalized (e.g., Gram–Schmidt), so we usually assume an orthonormal eigenbasis.
Completeness is crucial; in infinite dimensions it’s not always trivial to prove, so it’s taken as an axiom/restriction for “physical” observables: \[
\psi = \sum_n c_n\,\psi_n, \qquad c_n=\langle \psi_n,\psi\rangle.
\]


6.2 4.2 Continuous spectra: “Dirac orthonormality”
For operators with continuous spectra, eigenfunctions are typically not square-integrable (e.g., plane waves, delta functions). Still, we use a consistent extension:

Kronecker delta \(\delta_{mn}\) becomes Dirac delta \(\delta(z-z')\).
Sums become integrals.

Example: momentum operator \(\hat p=-i\hbar\,\partial_x\) has eigenfunctions \(e^{ipx/\hbar}\) (not normalizable), but we enforce: \[
\langle p|p'\rangle = \delta(p-p').
\]
Example: position operator \(\hat x\) has eigenfunctions \(\delta(x-y)\) with: \[
\langle x|x'\rangle = \delta(x-x').
\]
Completeness (continuous resolution of identity): \[
\int |z\rangle\langle z|\,dz = \hat I.
\]
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
7 5. Generalized statistical interpretation (measurement postulate)
This is the “measurement rule” in its most general form.

7.1 5.1 Possible outcomes
If you measure observable \(Q\) on a system in state \(|\psi\rangle\), the outcome must be one of the eigenvalues of \(\hat Q\).


7.2 5.2 Probabilities: discrete spectrum
If \(\hat Q\) has orthonormal eigenstates \(|q_n\rangle\), \[
|\psi\rangle = \sum_n c_n |q_n\rangle, \qquad c_n=\langle q_n|\psi\rangle,
\] then: \[
P(q_n)=|c_n|^2.
\]


7.3 5.3 Probabilities: continuous spectrum
If eigenstates \(|q\rangle\) satisfy Dirac orthonormality, \[
c(q)=\langle q|\psi\rangle,
\] then probability of landing in \([q,q+dq]\) is: \[
P(q\in[q,q+dq]) = |c(q)|^2\,dq.
\]


7.4 5.4 Collapse (state update)
After measuring \(Q\) and obtaining a value \(q\), the state “collapses” to the corresponding eigenstate (discrete: \(|q_n\rangle\); continuous: \(|q\rangle\), in an idealized sense).


7.5 5.5 Recovering position and momentum rules

Position basis: \(\psi(x)=\langle x|\psi\rangle\), and \(P(x\in[x,x+dx])=|\psi(x)|^2dx\).
Momentum basis: \(\phi(p)=\langle p|\psi\rangle\), and \(P(p\in[p,p+dp])=|\phi(p)|^2dp\).

The map \(\psi(x)\leftrightarrow \phi(p)\) is a Fourier transform: \[
\phi(p) \propto \int e^{-ipx/\hbar}\,\psi(x)\,dx,
\qquad
\psi(x) \propto \int e^{ipx/\hbar}\,\phi(p)\,dp.
\]
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
8 6. The uncertainty principle

8.1 6.1 Uncertainty as standard deviation
For any observable \(A\), \[
(\Delta A)^2 = \langle (\hat A-\langle A\rangle)^2\rangle.
\] Important: \(\Delta A\) does not mean your measurement device is imprecise; it means repeated measurements on identically prepared systems have an intrinsic spread.


8.2 6.2 Generalized uncertainty relation
Define \(\hat A'=\hat A-\langle A\rangle\), \(\hat B'=\hat B-\langle B\rangle\). Using Schwarz inequality with \(f=\hat A'\psi\), \(g=\hat B'\psi\), one obtains: \[
\Delta A\,\Delta B \;\ge\; \frac{1}{2}\,|\langle [\hat A,\hat B]\rangle|.
\] This is the generalized uncertainty principle.


8.3 6.3 Heisenberg uncertainty as a special case
For \(\hat x\) and \(\hat p\), \[
[\hat x,\hat p]=i\hbar
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\Delta x\,\Delta p \ge \frac{\hbar}{2}.
\]


8.4 6.4 Compatible vs incompatible observables

If \([\hat A,\hat B]=0\), observables are compatible and can have a complete set of simultaneous eigenstates.
If \([\hat A,\hat B]\neq 0\), they are incompatible and cannot (in general) be simultaneously determinate.

Operational viewpoint: a measurement that collapses the state into an eigenstate of \(A\) typically destroys any prior definiteness of \(B\), unless the observables are compatible.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
9 7. Minimum-uncertainty wave packets

9.1 7.1 When does equality hold?
To saturate the bound, the relevant vectors must be proportional: \[
\hat A'\psi = c\,\hat B'\psi
\] with the constant constrained so the “dropped” piece vanishes.


9.2 7.2 For \(x\) and \(p\): the Gaussian result
For the position-momentum case, the condition becomes a differential equation whose general solution is a Gaussian: \[
\psi(x)\propto \exp\!\big(-a(x-x_0)^2 + i k_0 x\big),
\] and these satisfy: \[
\Delta x\,\Delta p = \frac{\hbar}{2}.
\] So Gaussian wave packets are the most general minimum-uncertainty states (at a given instant).
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
10 8. Energy–time uncertainty (and why it’s different)

10.1 8.1 Time is not an operator (nonrelativistic QM)
Unlike \(x,p,E\), time \(t\) is the independent parameter in the Schrödinger equation, not an observable represented by an operator in the same way. So \(\Delta t\) is not a standard deviation of “time measurements.”


10.2 8.2 Generalized Ehrenfest theorem (commutator dynamics)
For an observable \(\hat Q\), \[
\frac{d}{dt}\langle Q\rangle = \frac{i}{\hbar}\langle [\hat H,\hat Q]\rangle
\quad \text{(if $\hat Q$ has no explicit $t$-dependence).}
\]


10.3 8.3 Mandelstam–Tamm form of energy–time uncertainty
Take the generalized uncertainty relation with \(A=H\) and \(B=Q\). Using the previous result, one obtains: \[
\Delta E\,\Delta t \gtrsim \frac{\hbar}{2},
\] where \(\Delta t\) is interpreted as a timescale for significant change in \(\langle Q\rangle\), e.g. \[
\Delta t \sim \frac{\Delta Q}{\left|d\langle Q\rangle/dt\right|}.
\] Key meaning: rapid evolution requires large energy spread; stationary states (\(\Delta E=0\)) have time-independent expectation values.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
section: 11 9. Vectors and operators: bases, matrices, and Dirac notation
text:
11 9. Vectors and operators: bases, matrices, and Dirac notation

11.1 9.1 States are abstract vectors (basis-independent)
The wavefunction \(\psi(x)\) is not the state itself; it’s the representation of the state in the position basis: \[
\psi(x) = \langle x|\psi\rangle.
\] Similarly, momentum-space wavefunction: \[
\phi(p)=\langle p|\psi\rangle.
\] And for a discrete energy basis \(\{|n\rangle\}\): \[
c_n = \langle n|\psi\rangle.
\] All contain the same information: they are just components in different bases.


11.2 9.2 Operators as linear transformations
Operators map vectors to vectors: \[
\hat A: |\psi\rangle \mapsto |\psi'\rangle.
\] Once a basis \(\{|n\rangle\}\) is chosen, the operator is represented by matrix elements: \[
A_{mn}=\langle m|\hat A|n\rangle.
\] Then components transform exactly like matrix multiplication.


11.3 9.3 Finite-dimensional systems (e.g., two-level system)
If the system admits only \(N\) linearly independent states, everything reduces to standard linear algebra:

state = \(N\)-component column vector,
observable = \(N\times N\) Hermitian matrix,
time evolution = solve Schrödinger equation with that matrix Hamiltonian.



11.4 9.4 Dirac notation (bras, kets, outer products)
Dirac splits the inner product “bracket”:

Ket \(|\psi\rangle\): a vector
Bra \(\langle \psi|\): a linear functional acting on kets to give a complex number: \[
\langle \phi|\psi\rangle.
\]

Outer product (rank-1 operator): \[
|\phi\rangle\langle \psi|
\] acts as: it “projects onto \(|\psi\rangle\)” and outputs along \(|\phi\rangle\) (up to normalization).
Projection operator onto a normalized \(|\alpha\rangle\): \[
\hat P_\alpha = |\alpha\rangle\langle \alpha|.
\]


11.5 9.5 Resolution of the identity (completeness)
For a discrete orthonormal basis: \[
\sum_n |n\rangle\langle n| = \hat I.
\] For a continuous basis: \[
\int |x\rangle\langle x|\,dx = \hat I,
\qquad
\int |p\rangle\langle p|\,dp = \hat I.
\]


11.6 9.6 Changing bases cleanly
Insert \(\hat I\) to transform components between bases: \[
\phi(p)=\langle p|\psi\rangle
      =\int \langle p|x\rangle\,\langle x|\psi\rangle\,dx
      =\int \langle p|x\rangle\,\psi(x)\,dx.
\] Recognizing \(\langle x|p\rangle\) as the momentum eigenfunction in \(x\)-space yields the Fourier transform.
Operators transform similarly: \[
\langle p|\hat x|\psi\rangle
= \int \langle p|x\rangle\,x\,\langle x|\psi\rangle\,dx,
\] leading to the momentum-space form of the position operator.
crumbs: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)

title: Hilbert-Space Formalism for Quantum Measurements (Griffiths Ch. 3 — Notes)
text:
12 10. One-page cheat sheet (key formulas)

12.1 States, probabilities, expectation values

\(\psi(x)=\langle x|\psi\rangle\)
\(P(x\in[x,x+dx])=|\psi(x)|^2dx\)
\(\langle A\rangle = \langle \psi|\hat A|\psi\rangle\)



12.2 Determinate states

\(\Delta A=0 \iff \hat A|\psi\rangle=a|\psi\rangle\)



12.3 Discrete spectral expansion

\(|\psi\rangle=\sum_n c_n|a_n\rangle\), \(c_n=\langle a_n|\psi\rangle\)
\(P(a_n)=|c_n|^2\)



12.4 Continuous spectral expansion

\(c(a)=\langle a|\psi\rangle\)

title: Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) on TCV
text:
Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) is an active spectroscopy diagnostic used in magnetically confined plasmas to measure density perturbations and their dynamics. The method relies on the injection of a neutral beam into the plasma and on the observation of the line radiation emitted by beam atoms after collisional excitation. In practice, BES isolates the beam-induced D\(\alpha\) emission and uses its spatio-temporal modulation as a proxy for local plasma fluctuations. This makes BES particularly valuable for the study of turbulence, magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) activity, correlation lengths, and fluctuation spectra.
In the present TCV context, the proposed BES implementation is based on observations of the Doppler-shifted D\(\alpha\) light from NBI-2. The design currently considers two complementary systems looking along the same beam: an imaging BES branch optimized for high spatial resolution and a fast-acquisition BES branch optimized for high temporal resolution.

title: Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) on TCV
text:
Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) is an active spectroscopy diagnostic used in magnetically confined plasmas to measure density perturbations and their dynamics. The method relies on the injection of a neutral beam into the plasma and on the observation of the line radiation emitted by beam atoms after collisional excitation. In practice, BES isolates the beam-induced D\(\alpha\) emission and uses its spatio-temporal modulation as a proxy for local plasma fluctuations. This makes BES particularly valuable for the study of turbulence, magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) activity, correlation lengths, and fluctuation spectra.
In the present TCV context, the proposed BES implementation is based on observations of the Doppler-shifted D\(\alpha\) light from NBI-2. The design currently considers two complementary systems looking along the same beam: an imaging BES branch optimized for high spatial resolution and a fast-acquisition BES branch optimized for high temporal resolution.

title: Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) on TCV
text:
2 Theoretical background

2.1 Principle of the measurement
When neutral deuterium from the beam penetrates the plasma, beam atoms are collisionally excited by plasma particles and subsequently emit Balmer radiation, in particular D\(\alpha\). The measured emissivity can be written schematically as
\[
\varepsilon_{D_\alpha} \propto n_b\,n_e\,\langle \sigma v \rangle_{\mathrm{exc}},
\]
where \(n_b\) is the local neutral beam density, \(n_e\) the electron density, and \(\langle \sigma v \rangle_{\mathrm{exc}}\) an effective excitation rate coefficient. Under conditions in which the beam properties and optical throughput are sufficiently stable, fluctuations of the detected signal can be related, to first order, to plasma density fluctuations:
\[
\frac{\delta I}{I} \approx C_n\frac{\delta n_e}{n_e} + C_T\frac{\delta T_e}{T_e} + C_b\frac{\delta n_b}{n_b} + \cdots
\]
In many BES applications, the dominant term of interest is the density-fluctuation contribution, while the remaining terms must be minimized, calibrated, or interpreted carefully.


2.2 Why Doppler-shifted D\(\alpha\) is used
A crucial experimental issue is the separation of beam-induced light from intense background plasma emission. Because the injected neutral beam has a large velocity, the D\(\alpha\) radiation produced by the beam is Doppler shifted relative to the passive D\(\alpha\) background. An ultra-narrow band-pass filter can therefore be tuned to transmit mainly the beam emission. This substantially improves the selectivity of the diagnostic and makes localized fluctuation measurements feasible.


2.3 Spatial and temporal information
BES does not measure a mathematical point value, but a signal integrated over a finite observation volume defined by the beam geometry, the line of sight, the optical system, and the atomic physics of excitation and emission. The diagnostic design is therefore a compromise between spatial localization, temporal resolution, and photon statistics:

high spatial resolution is needed to resolve short radial correlation lengths;
high temporal resolution is needed to resolve turbulence and MHD spectra;
sufficient photon flux is needed to maintain an acceptable signal-to-noise ratio.

This trade-off is precisely why the TCV concept is attractive in a dual configuration: one system emphasizes spatial detail, while the other emphasizes fast fluctuation dynamics.

title: Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) on TCV
text:
3 Historical overview
BES has become a standard active spectroscopy technique for fluctuation studies in tokamaks because it combines local sensitivity with direct access to density perturbations. Over time, BES implementations have evolved in two main directions. One direction uses arrays of discrete lines of sight and fast detectors to obtain high-bandwidth fluctuation measurements. The other direction pushes toward imaging approaches, in which a larger portion of the beam is observed simultaneously to recover finer spatial structure.
For the TCV project, this evolution is reflected directly in the two proposed concepts. The fast-acquisition BES design follows a proven line of development: according to the project presentation, similar diagnostic concepts have already been implemented on DIII-D, TFTR, MAST, and HL-2A. The imaging BES concept extends the diagnostic toward higher-resolution two-dimensional observation of the beam emissivity pattern, with the aim of improving spatial characterization and enabling simultaneous low-field-side and high-field-side measurements.

title: Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) on TCV
Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) on TCV
text:
5 Outlook
From a diagnostic point of view, the proposed BES development on TCV is attractive because it couples a relatively straightforward integration path with a broad physics reach. The imaging branch appears especially promising for high-resolution spatial studies, whereas the fast-acquisition branch builds on an already established BES tradition in other devices. If both concepts are implemented successfully, TCV would gain a versatile diagnostic set for fluctuation studies along NBI-2, spanning both fine spatial localization and high-bandwidth temporal analysis.

title: Neutral Beam Injector (NBI)
text: Neutral Beam Injection (NBI) is a key technique used in tokamak fusion devices to heat the plasma and drive current. In NBI systems, neutral atoms—typically hydrogen or deuterium—are accelerated to high energies and injected into the plasma. Because charged particles would be deflected by the magnetic fields in the tokamak, the ions are first neutralized before injection. Once inside the plasma, these fast neutrals transfer their energy to the plasma particles through collisions, increasing the plasma temperature and contributing to the overall performance of the fusion experiment. NBI is valued for its ability to deliver precise and controllable heating, making it an essential tool in plasma physics research and fusion reactor development.

title: Neutral Beam Injector (NBI)
text: NBI modelling

title: Neutral Beam Injector (NBI)
text:
Generalization to Astigmatic Beam Optics
The standard analytical model for the beam current density (Eq. 5) relies on the Modified Bessel function of the first kind, \(I_0\), which assumes perfect cylindrical symmetry. However, the neutral beam injector exhibits astigmatism, characterized by distinct divergence angles (\(\theta_{0x}, \theta_{0y}\)) and focal lengths (\(R_x, R_y\)) for the horizontal and vertical axes.
To account for this asymmetry, we cannot perform the angular integration analytically. Instead, we revert to the Cartesian representation. While the physics of the beam is separable into \(x\) and \(y\) components, the geometric constraints of the grid require integrating over a circular aperture of radius \(a\).
The generalized current density \(j_{\text{beam}}(x, y, z)\) is therefore expressed as a double integral where the integration limits are coupled to represent the circular source, but the kernel allows for independent focusing parameters:
\[
\begin{aligned}
j_{\text{beam}}(x, y, z) &= B \underbrace{\exp \left[ -\left( \frac{x^2}{z^2 \theta_{0x}^2} + \frac{y^2}{z^2 \theta_{0y}^2} \right) \right]}_{\text{Observation Plane Profile}} \\
&\quad \times \int_{-a}^{a} dx' \int_{-\sqrt{a^2 - x'^2}}^{+\sqrt{a^2 - x'^2}} dy' \\
&\quad \times \underbrace{\exp \left[ - \frac{x'^2}{\theta_{0x}^2} \left( \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{R_x} \right)^2 + \frac{2 x x'}{z \theta_{0x}^2} \left( \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{R_x} \right) \right]}_{\text{Source-Target Interaction (x-axis)}} \\
&\quad \times \underbrace{\exp \left[ - \frac{y'^2}{\theta_{0y}^2} \left( \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{R_y} \right)^2 + \frac{2 y y'}{z \theta_{0y}^2} \left( \frac{1}{z} - \frac{1}{R_y} \right) \right]}_{\text{Source-Target Interaction (y-axis)}}
\end{aligned}
\]
In this formulation:

Integration Domain: The outer integral over \(x'\) combined with the inner integral limits \(\pm\sqrt{a^2 - x'^2}\) strictly defines the circular aperture of the grid.
Beam Steering: The cross-terms (e.g., \(2xx'\))—which previously collapsed into the Bessel function—are now explicit, preserving the phase information required to model different focal lengths \(R_x\) and \(R_y\).

title: Neutral Beam Injector (NBI)
text:
Numerical Implementation
To evaluate the generalized current density integral efficiently in MATLAB, we utilize a semi-analytical approach. The double integral over the circular source shown above is computationally expensive if solved via 2D quadrature for every voxel.
However, the Gaussian nature of the beamlet profiles allows us to solve the inner integral analytically. The code function beam_propagation implements this by decomposing the integration into a numerical outer loop and an analytical inner step.

1. Decomposition of the Integral
The double integral over the circular aperture \(x'^2 + y'^2 \le a^2\) can be separated. If we fix \(x'\) (the loop variable x0 in the code), the limits for \(y'\) are \(\pm \sqrt{a^2 - x'^2}\).
The term dependent on \(y'\) in the integrand is essentially a Gaussian. The integral of a Gaussian over finite limits is given by the Error Function (\(\text{erf}\)).
\[\int_{-Y_{lim}}^{+Y_{lim}} e^{-c_1 y'^2 + c_2 y'} dy' \propto \left[ \text{erf}\left( \dots \right) \right]_{-Y_{lim}}^{+Y_{lim}}\]


2. The beam_propagation Algorithm
The code approximates the generalized current density formula using this optimization:

Discretization: The source radius \(a\) (source_radius) is discretized into \(N\) strips (steps) along the \(x'\)-axis (variable x0).
[cite_start]Vectorization: Geometric factors dependent on \(z\) (beam coordinate \(s\)) and \(R\) (focal length) are pre-calculated outside the loop[cite: 76, 79].
Semi-Analytical Integration: * The outer loop sums the contribution of each \(x'\) strip.

The inner integration over \(y'\) is replaced by the difference of error functions: erf(arg_A) - erf(arg_B).


This reduces the complexity from \(O(N^2)\) to \(O(N)\), allowing the simulator to compute millions of voxels in seconds. [cite_start]This method is consistent with the approach of summing elementary beam contributions described in the reference model[cite: 42, 86].

title: Neutral Beam Injector (NBI)
text:
Beam Attenuation Model
While the geometric focusing determines the beam’s shape, the plasma interaction determines its intensity decay. The simulator models this attenuation using the Beer-Lambert law along the beam path.

1. Optical Depth Calculation
The local attenuation coefficient \(\alpha\) is defined as the product of the electron density and the effective stopping cross-section:
\[\alpha(s) = n_e(s) \cdot \sigma_{\text{eff}}(E, n_e, T_e)\]
The total attenuation (Optical Depth \(\tau\)) at a distance \(s\) is the cumulative integral of \(\alpha\) along the path:
\[\tau(s) = \int_{0}^{s} \alpha(s') \, ds'\]


2. Fast Integration Scheme
In the NBI_simulator code, this physics is implemented using a vectorized “Fast Integration” scheme:

Grid Generation: A 2D planar grid (S_grid, T_grid) is generated along the beam axis.
Vectorized Mapping: Plasma profiles (\(n_e, T_e\)) are mapped onto this 2D sheet.
cumtrapz Acceleration: Instead of using an iterative solver (like integral), the code uses MATLAB’s cumulative trapezoidal integration cumtrapz. This computes the attenuation for the entire beam cross-section simultaneously.



3. Wall Interaction
The model includes a geometric “hard stop” condition for the inner wall of the tokamak. \[\text{If } R_{\text{major}} < (R_0 - a) \implies \alpha \to \infty\] This ensures that any portion of the beam striking the high-field side protection tiles is fully absorbed and does not artificially reappear on the other side of the vessel.

title: Neutral Beam Injector (NBI)
text:
Particle Density Scaling
The simulator resolves the beam into three energy components: Full (\(E\)), Half (\(E/2\)), and Third (\(E/3\)). A critical physical scaling used in the code is that lower energy components often have higher particle densities, even if their power fraction is lower.
The particle density \(n\) is derived from the power \(P\) and particle velocity \(v\):
\[I_{\text{beam}} = \frac{P}{E} \quad \text{(Particle Flux)}\] \[n = \frac{I_{\text{beam}}}{v} = \frac{P}{E \cdot v}\]
Since \(v \propto \sqrt{E}\), the density scales as:
\[n \propto \frac{P}{E^{3/2}}\]
This \(E^{-3/2}\) dependence means that the Half-Energy component travels significantly slower than the Full-Energy component, causing particles to “pile up” and resulting in a higher local density for the same power flux.

title: CMOS, CCD and EMCCD detectors for BES imaging
text:
For an imaging Beam Emission Spectroscopy (BES) system, the choice of detector is not a secondary hardware detail: it directly determines the achievable frame rate, exposure time, noise level, image uniformity, and analysis strategy. In practice, the detector must be matched to the physics objective. If the goal is to obtain very uniform, low-noise images under photon-starved conditions, CCD-based solutions can be attractive. If the goal is to acquire images at high frame rate with short exposure time, CMOS cameras are generally more suitable.
For the TCV BES imaging concept, the relevant comparison is therefore not simply “which camera is better?”, but rather which detector architecture is better matched to a high-speed fluctuation diagnostic.

title: CMOS, CCD and EMCCD detectors for BES imaging
section: 2 How CCD and CMOS sensors differ
text:
2 How CCD and CMOS sensors differ

2.1 CCD principle
A charge-coupled device (CCD) stores the photo-generated charge in each pixel during the exposure, and then transfers that charge across the chip toward a small number of output amplifiers. In a classical CCD, many pixels are therefore read through the same output chain.
This architecture has an important consequence: because the readout path is highly centralized, the pixel response is often very uniform and the fixed-pattern background can be well controlled. This is one reason why CCDs have historically been very attractive in low-light scientific imaging, including astronomy.
The drawback is that the serial or quasi-serial transfer of charge makes the readout comparatively slow. In addition, during readout, charge transfer can lead to image smearing unless the sensor or the acquisition scheme is specifically designed to suppress it.


2.2 EMCCD as a special CCD case
An electron-multiplying CCD (EMCCD) is a special CCD architecture that introduces a multiplication register before the final amplifier. This makes it possible to amplify very weak signals before the read noise is added, which is especially useful in extremely low-light applications.
EMCCDs therefore retain many of the classical CCD advantages in terms of uniform readout and sensitivity, but they are typically more expensive and are not the natural choice when very high frame rate over a reasonably large image area is the primary requirement.


2.3 CMOS principle
A CMOS image sensor also integrates photo-generated charge in each pixel, but the readout architecture is fundamentally different. In CMOS sensors, pixels are addressed more directly, and amplification and readout functions are distributed at the pixel or column level rather than being concentrated into one single output chain.
This parallelized architecture is the main reason why CMOS cameras are generally much faster than CCD cameras. It also enables a cleaner decoupling between exposure and readout, so short exposures can be combined with high acquisition frequency without the same degree of readout smearing associated with conventional CCD operation.
The price to pay is that the readout is less intrinsically uniform: column-to-column or pixel-to-pixel offsets, fixed-pattern noise, and a more structured background are more common, so the analysis pipeline usually requires more image correction and background processing.

title: Optics
text: Optics is the branch of physics which studies how the electromagnetic radiation behaves and how it can be manipulated. The most common use of optics is the geometric optics, which considers the light as travelling in straight lines, and bending when passing through or reflect from surfaces. Physical optics instead considers light as an electromagnetic wave, and more complex effects can be explained, such as diffraction and interference, which cannot be taken into account by geometrical optics.

title: Optics
text: Optics is the branch of physics which studies how the electromagnetic radiation behaves and how it can be manipulated. The most common use of optics is the geometric optics, which considers the light as travelling in straight lines, and bending when passing through or reflect from surfaces. Physical optics instead considers light as an electromagnetic wave, and more complex effects can be explained, such as diffraction and interference, which cannot be taken into account by geometrical optics.

title: Optics
text:
Refraction and reflection
When a ray hits the boundary between two materials which are transparent, it is dived into a reflected and refracted ray.
Both the reflected and refracted rays stay on the plane of incidence but, while the former is reflected with the same incidence angle (respect to the normal to the interface) \(\theta_1\), the latter is refracted with an angle \(\theta_2\) according to Snell’s Law:
\[
\frac{\sin\theta_1}{\sin\theta_2} = \frac{n_2}{n_1},
\]
where \(n_2\) and \(n_1\) are respectively the refractive index of the second and the first material. The index of refraction is strictly connected to the ligth speed in the medium \(v\):
\[
n = \frac{c}{v},
\]
where \(c\) is the speed of light in vacuum.
When \(\theta_1\) is large enough, the light is fully reflected and no transmission occurs. This phenomenon is the one used by the optical fibers to transmit optical signals over long distances.

title: Optics
text:
Interference
Interference happens when different waves interact and generate a patterns which depends on the waves wavelengths and frequencies and wether they are in phase or not. In the former case the outcome is called a constructive interference, and the resulting wave has a higher amplitude (brighter light); if instead the two waves are out of phase, the resulting amplitude is smaller than the original one, and the phenomenon is called distructive interference.

title: Optics
text:
Diffraction
Diffraction happens when light, after passing through a number of slits, interferes with its own wavefronts. The diffraction grating contains a large number of slits at equal spacing.

title: Hermitian adjoint
text:
The Hermitian adjoint of a matrix or operator \(\mathbf{A}\), often written as \(\mathbf{A}^\dagger\), especially when used in conjunction with the bra-ket notation, is defined as the complex conjugate transpose of \(\mathbf{A}\). Mathematically, if \(\mathbf{A}\) is an \(m \times n\) matrix, then
\[
\mathbf{A}^\dagger = (\mathbf{A}^*)^T
\]
where \(\mathbf{A}^*\) denotes the element-wise complex conjugate and \(T\) denotes the transpose.
In quantum mechanics, the Hermitian adjoint plays a crucial role in defining observables and ensuring that physical quantities are real-valued. An operator \(\mathbf{A}\) is called Hermitian if \(\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}^\dagger\). Hermitian operators have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors, which are essential properties for representing measurable quantities.
For example, if \(\mathbf{A}\) acts on a vector space with basis vectors \(|i\rangle\), the matrix elements of the Hermitian adjoint are given by: \[
\langle i | \mathbf{A}^\dagger | j \rangle = \langle j | \mathbf{A} | i \rangle^*
\]
This property is widely used in the manipulation of quantum states and operators.

title: Bra-ket notation
text:
The bra-ket notation, also called Dirac notation, is designed to make some operations of quantum mechanics easier. The notation uses angle brackets to denote vectors in a complex Hilbert space.
A ket is written as \(|\psi\rangle\) and represents a column vector (state vector), while a bra, written as \(\langle\phi|\) represents the conjugate transpose (row vector) of a ket.
The inner product (overlap) between two states is written as \[
\langle\phi|\psi\rangle,
\]
and in quantum mechanics this expression is interpreted as the probability amplitude for a state \(\phi\) to collapse onto \(\psi\) (the state \(\phi\) is projected onto the state \(\psi\)).
The outer product, which forms an operator, is written as: \[
|\psi\rangle\langle\phi|.
\]

title: About the author
text:
Hi!
My name is Luigi Persico, I am 25 and I am from Italy. Currently I am doing a Ph.D in Plasma Physics at EPFL, and here you can have a look at what I am working on.
Enjoy ;)

title: Derivation of Wave Energy Density
text:
Goal: Derive the expression for the time-averaged energy density of a monochromatic wave in a dispersive medium: \[
\langle W \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \left[ \vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial(\omega \hat{\epsilon})}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{E}_0 + \vec{H}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial(\omega \hat{\mu})}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{H}_0 \right]
\]


We start with the conservation of energy for electromagnetic fields (Poynting’s Theorem). The rate of work done by the fields on the current density \(\vec{J}\) is: \[
\vec{J} \cdot \vec{E} = - \nabla \cdot (\vec{E} \times \vec{H}) - \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} - \vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
\] The term \(\vec{J} \cdot \vec{E}\) represents the power absorbed by the medium. We are interested in the energy stored in a lossless wave packet, so we look at the time rate of change of energy density \(\frac{\partial W}{\partial t}\).
For a dispersive medium, we cannot simply write \(W = \frac{1}{2}(\vec{E}\cdot\vec{D} + \vec{B}\cdot\vec{H})\). Instead, we must analyze a wave packet that grows slowly in time to accumulate energy.



Consider a quasi-monochromatic wave with a slowly varying amplitude \(\vec{E}_0(t)\) (slow compared to the frequency \(\omega\)). The electric field is: \[
\vec{E}(t) = \text{Re} \left[ \vec{E}_0(t) e^{-i\omega t} \right]
\] The displacement field \(\vec{D}\) is related to \(\vec{E}\) by the permittivity \(\hat{\epsilon}(\omega)\). Since the amplitude is time-dependent, we expand \(\hat{\epsilon}\) around the carrier frequency \(\omega\) using a Taylor series for the time derivative operator \(\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \to -i\omega + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\) acting on the envelope.
However, a cleaner approach is to work in the frequency domain. The displacement field is: \[
\vec{D}(t) = \text{Re} \left[ \hat{\epsilon}(\omega) \vec{E}_0(t) e^{-i\omega t} + i \frac{\partial \hat{\epsilon}}{\partial \omega} \frac{d\vec{E}_0}{dt} e^{-i\omega t} + \dots \right]
\] (The second term arises because the frequency spectrum of a varying pulse is not a delta function; the derivative \(\partial \epsilon / \partial \omega\) accounts for the “spread” of frequencies).



We calculate the electric contribution to the power density term \(\vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}\). Using the complex notation \(A(t) = \frac{1}{2}(A_0 e^{-i\omega t} + A_0^* e^{i\omega t})\): \[
\langle \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \left( \vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}_0}{\partial t} + \vec{E}_0 \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}_0^*}{\partial t} \right)
\] where \(\vec{D}_0 \approx \epsilon(\omega) \vec{E}_0\). However, explicitly keeping the slow time variation allows us to identify the stored energy. The dominant term for energy accumulation involves the time derivative of the amplitude.
Let’s evaluate \(\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}\) keeping the first order correction: \[
\frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \approx \text{Re} \left[ -i\omega \epsilon \vec{E}_0 e^{-i\omega t} + \frac{\partial (\omega \epsilon)}{\partial \omega} \frac{d\vec{E}_0}{dt} e^{-i\omega t} \right]
\] Now, take the dot product with \(\vec{E}(t)\) and time average over one period \(T = 2\pi/\omega\). Fast oscillating terms like \(e^{-2i\omega t}\) average to zero. We are left with cross terms involving \(\vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{d\vec{E}_0}{dt}\).
The time-averaged rate of change of energy is: \[
\left\langle \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right\rangle = \frac{1}{4} \vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial (\omega \epsilon)}{\partial \omega} \cdot \frac{d\vec{E}_0}{dt} + \text{c.c.}
\] Assuming the medium is lossless (Hermitian \(\epsilon\)), this can be rewritten as a total time derivative: \[
\left\langle \vec{E} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \right\rangle = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \left( \frac{1}{4} \vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial (\omega \epsilon)}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{E}_0 \right)
\]



The quantity inside the time derivative is the stored electric energy density. \[
\langle W_E \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial (\omega \epsilon)}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{E}_0
\] An identical derivation applies to the magnetic term \(\vec{H} \cdot \frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}\), yielding: \[
\langle W_M \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \vec{H}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial (\omega \mu)}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{H}_0
\] Summing these gives the total wave energy density (Eq 12.1).

title: Derivation of Wave Energy Density
text:

\langle W \rangle = \frac{1}{4} \left[ \vec{E}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial(\omega \hat{\epsilon})}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{E}_0 + \vec{H}_0^* \cdot \frac{\partial(\omega \hat{\mu})}{\partial \omega} \cdot \vec{H}_0 \right]

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
To analyze stability, MHD variables are decomposed into a static equilibrium component and a small perturbation (\(Q \rightarrow Q + \delta Q\)). Assuming negligible equilibrium fluid velocity (\(u=0\)), the perturbed velocity is defined by the fluid displacement \(\xi\) as \(\delta u = \partial\xi/\partial t\).

Momentum Equation: \(\rho \frac{\partial^2\xi}{\partial t^2} = \delta F(\xi)\), where \(\delta F\) is the linearized force operator.
Perturbed Pressure: \(\delta P = -\gamma P \nabla \cdot \xi - \xi \cdot \nabla P\). This includes an adiabatic .
Perturbed Magnetic Field: \(\delta B = \nabla \times (\xi \times B)\), derived from Faraday’s and Ohm’s laws.
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
To analyze stability, MHD variables are decomposed into a static equilibrium component and a small perturbation (\(Q \rightarrow Q + \delta Q\)). Assuming negligible equilibrium fluid velocity (\(u=0\)), the perturbed velocity is defined by the fluid displacement \(\xi\) as \(\delta u = \partial\xi/\partial t\).

Momentum Equation: \(\rho \frac{\partial^2\xi}{\partial t^2} = \delta F(\xi)\), where \(\delta F\) is the linearized force operator.
Perturbed Pressure: \(\delta P = -\gamma P \nabla \cdot \xi - \xi \cdot \nabla P\). This includes an adiabatic .
Perturbed Magnetic Field: \(\delta B = \nabla \times (\xi \times B)\), derived from Faraday’s and Ohm’s laws.
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
2. The Energy Principle and \(\delta W\)
The energy principle is the most efficient way to determine stability without solving for exact eigenvalues. Because the linear force operator \(\delta F\) is self-adjoint, energy is conserved: \(\delta K + \delta W = \text{constant}\).

Kinetic Energy (\(\delta K\)): \(\frac{1}{2} \int d^3x \rho |\dot{\xi}|^2\).
Potential Energy (\(\delta W\)): \(-\frac{1}{2} \int d^3x \xi^* \cdot \delta F(\xi)\).
Stability Criterion: An equilibrium is stable if and only if \(\delta W > 0\) for all allowable displacements \(\xi\).
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
3. Physical Components of \(\delta W\)
The potential energy \(\delta W\) can be decomposed into intuitive physical terms:

Field Line Bending: Always stabilizing; represents magnetic energy in Alfvén waves.
Magnetic Compression: Always stabilizing; corresponds to energy needed to compress the magnetic field.
Pressure Drive: Potential energy for ballooning and interchange modes. It is destabilizing in regions of “unfavourable curvature” where \(\nabla P\) and curvature \(\kappa\) are parallel.
Current Drive: Free energy from parallel equilibrium current (\(J_{||}\)), responsible for kink instabilities.
Plasma Compression: Energy required to compress the plasma fluid, which is stabilizing.
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
4. Incompressibility and Marginal Stability
Near the stability boundary (marginal stability), instabilities are nearly incompressible (\(\nabla \cdot \xi \approx 0\)).

In this limit, \(\delta W\) can be minimized with respect to parallel displacement \(\xi_{||}\) by setting the plasma compression term to zero.
This reduces the complexity of stability analysis, allowing \(\delta W\) to be expressed in terms of perpendicular displacement \(\xi_{\perp}\) alone.
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
5. Inverse Aspect Ratio Expansion
Stability equations are often expanded in the inverse aspect ratio \(\epsilon = r/R_0\).

Second Order (\(\delta W_2\)): At this order, internal modes are stable in ideal MHD because field line bending is strongly stabilizing. Cylinder and torus stability are identical at this order.
Fourth Order (\(\delta W_4\)): Necessary for analyzing the \(m=n=1\) internal kink mode, where toroidal effects and the Shafranov shift become crucial.
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability
text:
6. The \(m=n=1\) Internal Kink Mode
This mode is a special case where field line bending vanishes in the core.

It is marginally stable to \(O(\epsilon^2)\).
Toroidal effects are so dominant that they entirely cancel the fourth-order cylindrical stabilization terms for \(n=1\).
The result is a “rigidly shifted” core region where \(q < 1\), effectively creating a new tilted toroidal equilibrium.
crumbs: Lecture 3: Theory of Linear Ideal MHD Stability

title: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria
text:
Ideal MHD is used to assess the “lowest level” of stability for magnetized plasmas. While it assumes the plasma is collisional, it effectively describes macroscopic perpendicular dynamics (cross-field behavior).

Strengths: It is a closed system that allows for the study of complex non-linear effects.
Limitations: It is a poor descriptor of dynamics parallel to the magnetic field in collisionless plasmas, as these occur too rapidly for the model.
Marginal Stability: Near marginal stability (small growth rates), plasma motions are considered incompressible.
crumbs: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria

title: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria
text:
Ideal MHD is used to assess the “lowest level” of stability for magnetized plasmas. While it assumes the plasma is collisional, it effectively describes macroscopic perpendicular dynamics (cross-field behavior).

Strengths: It is a closed system that allows for the study of complex non-linear effects.
Limitations: It is a poor descriptor of dynamics parallel to the magnetic field in collisionless plasmas, as these occur too rapidly for the model.
Marginal Stability: Near marginal stability (small growth rates), plasma motions are considered incompressible.
crumbs: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria

title: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria
text:
2. Conservation of Flux (Frozen-in Law)
In the Ideal limit (resistivity \(\eta = 0\)), the magnetic field topology is invariant.

Ideal Ohm’s Law: Defined as \(E + u \times B = 0\).
Physical Intuition: Magnetic field lines are conceptually “frozen” into the plasma fluid.
Mechanism: The flux linking a loop moving with the fluid velocity is constant, meaning the fluid and field lines must move together.
crumbs: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria

title: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria
text:
3. Equilibrium Force Balance
Static equilibrium (\(u = 0\)) requires the Lorentz force to balance the pressure gradient: \[J \times B = \nabla P\]

Isobaric Surfaces: Magnetic field lines (\(B \cdot \nabla P = 0\)) and current lines (\(J \cdot \nabla P = 0\)) lie on surfaces of constant pressure.
Flux Surfaces: In a tokamak, these constant pressure surfaces are known as flux surfaces.
Toroidal Topology: The “Hairy Ball” theorem states that only a toroidal surface can be covered by a non-vanishing vector field like \(B\) and \(J\).
crumbs: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria

title: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria
text:
4. The Grad-Shafranov Equation (GSE)
The GSE is a non-linear, second-order elliptic partial differential equation that describes axisymmetric toroidal equilibria.
\[\Delta^* \psi = -R^2 \frac{dP(\psi)}{d\psi} - F(\psi) \frac{dF(\psi)}{d\psi}\]

Key Components to Understand:

Poloidal Flux (\(\psi\)): A stream function used to represent field components in the poloidal plane.
Free Functions: To solve the GSE, one must prescribe the pressure profile \(P(\psi)\) and the toroidal field/current function \(F(\psi) = RB_\phi\).
The Operator (\(\Delta^*\)): Defined as \(R^2 \nabla \cdot (\frac{1}{R^2} \nabla)\) or, in cylindrical coordinates, \(R \frac{\partial}{\partial R}(\frac{1}{R} \frac{\partial}{\partial R}) + \frac{\partial^2}{\partial Z^2}\).
crumbs: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria

title: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria
text:
5. Solving the Equilibrium Problem
The essence of the problem is solving for the locations of constant \(\psi\) surfaces over the \((R, Z)\) plane.

Fixed Boundary: Defining \(\psi\) on a closed contour, often assuming the boundary is a perfect conductor where \(\psi\) is constant.
Free Boundary: Involves specifying flux in the vacuum region and accounting for external coil currents.
Iteration: Realistic free boundary problems require iterative procedures because the plasma current distribution depends on the resulting equilibrium shape.
crumbs: Lecture 1: The Ideal MHD Model and Axisymmetric Equilibria

title: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment
text:
External kink modes are instabilities where the perturbed magnetic field extends beyond the plasma boundary into the vacuum region.

Destabilization Source: Unlike internal modes, which are stable at second order in the inverse aspect ratio, surface corrections at \(O(\epsilon^2)\) can be destabilizing for external kinks.
Boundary Condition: These modes satisfy \(\xi^r(a) \neq 0\), meaning the plasma-vacuum interface itself is displaced.
Vacuum Energy: Stability is determined by the total potential energy \(\delta W = \delta W_P + \delta W_V\).
Vacuum Perturbation: The vacuum field \(\delta \hat{B}\) is found by solving \(\nabla \cdot \delta \hat{B} = 0\) and \(\nabla \times \delta \hat{B} = 0\), coupled to the plasma displacement at the interface.
crumbs: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment

title: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment
text:

title: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment
section: 2. The Role of a Conducting Wall
text:
2. The Role of a Conducting Wall
A conducting wall placed at radius \(r = b\) (where \(b > a\)) has a profound effect on the stability of external modes.

Flux Compression: In the ideal limit, the wall acts as a perfect conductor where the normal component of the perturbed field must vanish (\(n \cdot \delta \hat{B}|_b = 0\)).
Stabilizing Force: This constraint compresses the poloidal flux between the plasma and the wall, providing a restorative force.
Resistive Wall Modes (RWM): In real devices, walls have finite resistivity. The stabilizing flux can only remain compressed for a “skin time,” after which the mode can grow at a rate determined by the wall’s resistance.
crumbs: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment

title: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment
text:
3. Inertia and Growth Rates
While the Energy Principle determines if a mode is unstable, calculating the growth rate \(\gamma\) (where \(\xi \sim e^{\gamma t}\)) requires including the kinetic energy \(\delta K\).

Normal Mode Equation: \(-\omega^2 \rho \xi = \delta F(\xi)\).
Eigenvalue Relation: \(\omega^2 = \delta W / K\), where \(\omega^2 < 0\) corresponds to a purely growing instability (\(\gamma^2 = -\omega^2\)).
Inertia Renormalization: Inclusion of the parallel displacement \(\xi_{||}\) in the inertia term can renormalize the growth rate. For nearly incompressible instabilities, \(\gamma^2\) is scaled by a factor of \((1 + 2q^2)\).
crumbs: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment

title: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment
text:
4. The Layer Region and Singularity
For modes like the internal kink, the ideal MHD equations become singular at the rational surface where \(q = m/n\).

Layer Treatment: In a narrow region (the “layer”) around the singularity, ideal MHD assumptions break down, and effects like inertia, resistivity, or kinetic physics must be restored.
Matching: The solution is found by matching the “outer” ideal MHD solution to the “inner” layer solution.
Internal Kink Scaling: For the \(m=n=1\) internal kink, the minimization of total energy reveals that the kinetic energy \(K\) scales as \(1/\omega\), leading to a growth rate \(\gamma \propto \delta W\) rather than \(\gamma \propto \sqrt{\delta W}\).
crumbs: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment

title: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment
text:
5. Summary of Stability Analysis Methods
The lecture contrasts different computational and analytical approaches:

Initial Value Problem: Solves the full evolution; consistent with non-linear treatment but computationally expensive.
Normal Mode Method: Solves for eigenvalues \(\omega\); requires solving coupled partial differential equations.
Energy Principle: The most efficient for marginal stability; checks if any allowable displacement makes \(\delta W < 0\).
crumbs: Lecture 4: External Kink Modes and Inertia Treatment

title: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities
text:
Lecture 7 explores how high-energy “fast” ions (from Neutral Beam Injection or alpha particles) modify standard MHD stability. While the bulk plasma carries the fluid momentum, fast ions introduce kinetic effects and pressure anisotropy that alter the equilibrium and potential energy of the system.

Extended Momentum Equation: The perpendicular momentum equation is modified to include terms for anisotropic pressure (\(\delta P_{\perp}\) and \(\delta P_{||}\)) and magnetic curvature \(\kappa\).
Quasi-neutrality: The model assumes \(\sum q_j \delta n_j = 0\), where the sum includes both thermal species and fast ions.
Leading Order Fluid Motion: The perpendicular velocity remains dominated by the \(E \times B\) drift.
crumbs: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities

title: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities
text:

title: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities
text:
2. Kinetic-MHD Potential Energy (\(\delta W\))
The stability criterion is expanded to include a kinetic contribution from the fast particles, \(\delta W_h\): \[\delta W = \delta W_c + \delta W_h\] where \(\delta W_c\) is the standard “core” fluid MHD energy and \(\delta W_h\) represents the fast particle response.

Adiabatic Response: Fast particles provide an “adiabatic” pressure response that is typically stabilizing.
Kinetic Response: This accounts for the resonant interactions between the mode frequency and the orbital frequencies (bounce or precession) of the fast ions.
crumbs: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities

title: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities
text:
3. Fast Particle Distribution and Curvature
The stabilization effect depends heavily on how the fast ions are distributed in phase space and their interaction with magnetic curvature.

Pressure Averaged Curvature: Stabilization occurs when the fast ion pressure gradient is coupled to favorable magnetic curvature.
Trapped vs. Passing Particles: Trapped particles often have a more significant impact on stability because their “precession” frequency can resonate with long-wavelength MHD modes like the internal kink.
crumbs: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities

title: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities
section: 4. Impact on the \(m=1\) Internal Kink Mode
text:
4. Impact on the \(m=1\) Internal Kink Mode
Fast ions have a dual role in the stability of the \(m=1\) internal kink mode (the mode responsible for “sawtooth” oscillations):

Destabilizing Effect: They can increase the total poloidal beta (\(\beta_p\)), which enhances the toroidal drive for the instability.
Stabilizing Effect: The kinetic response (\(\delta W_h\)) provides a powerful restorative force that can “stabilize” the mode even when \(q < 1\) in the core.
Sawtooth Period Extension: This stabilization explains why the presence of fast ions (e.g., from NBI) often leads to much longer intervals between sawtooth crashes.
crumbs: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities

title: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities
text:
5. Modified Mercier Criterion
The presence of fast particles also modifies the local stability criteria for interchange modes. The standard Mercier criterion is adjusted to account for the kinetic pressure of the hot species, which can provide an additional “magnetic well” effect or modify the average curvature term.

Key Research Takeaway: Fast particles are not just an “extra” population; they are a critical control tool for stability. By tailoring the fast ion distribution, researchers can suppress long-wavelength instabilities, though this can lead to larger “monster” sawtooth crashes when the stabilization limit is finally exceeded.
crumbs: Lecture 7: Fast Ion Effects on Pressure Driven Instabilities

title: Exercise 2: Grad-Shafranov Expansion and Shaping
text:
The lecture notes adopt the coordinate system \((r, \omega, \phi)\) for an inverse aspect ratio expansion and Fourier representation of the cylindrical coordinates \((R, Z, \phi)\):
\[
R(r, \omega) = R_0 \left( 1 + \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon} \epsilon \cos \omega - \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} \frac{\Delta(r)}{R_0} + \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} \sum_{m=2}^{\infty} \frac{S_m(r)}{R_0} \cos(m-1)\omega + \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^3} \frac{\mathcal{P}(r)}{R_0} \cos \omega + O(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^4}) \right)
\]
\[
Z(r, \omega) = R_0 \left( \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon} \epsilon \sin \omega - \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} \sum_{m=2}^{\infty} \frac{S_m(r)}{R_0} \sin(m-1)\omega + \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^3} \frac{\mathcal{P}(r)}{R_0} \sin \omega + O(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^4}) \right)
\]
where the red epsilon \(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon}\) is an artificial tag indicating the ordering of each term. The Fourier coefficients of the Grad-Shafranov equation are:
\(\cos(0\omega)\) mode: \[
\left[ \frac{1}{2r^2} (r^2 \psi'^2)' + R_0^2 P' + FF' \right] [1 + O(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon})] = 0 \tag{3}
\]
\(\cos(\omega)\) mode: \[
\left[ \Delta'' + \left( 2\frac{\psi''}{\psi'} + \frac{1}{r} \right)\Delta' - \frac{1}{R_0} + 2 \frac{rR_0P'}{(\psi')^2} \right] [1 + O(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon})] = 0 \tag{4}
\]
\(\cos(m\omega)\) mode: \[
\left[ S_m'' + \left( 2\frac{\psi''}{\psi'} + \frac{1}{r} \right)S_m' + \frac{1-m^2}{r^2} S_m \right] [1 + O(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon})] = 0 \tag{5}
\]


Show (e.g. by substitution) that on setting \(\psi' = rF(r)/(R_0 q(r))\), \(P/B^2 \sim \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2}\), \(s \sim 1\) the solution to Eq. (3) is:
\[
F = R_0 B_0 (1 + \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} F_2(r)), \quad F_2(r) = - \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^{-2}} \frac{P}{B_0^2} - \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^{-2}} \int_0^r \frac{dr \ r}{R_0^2} \left( \frac{2-s}{q^2} \right) + O(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^4}) \tag{6}
\]
Explain from this result how the plasma can cause a well in the magnetic field strength (compared to the field strength in a vacuum).



Integrate Eq. (4) once, adopt again \(\psi' = rF(r)/(R_0 q(r))\), to show that to leading order:
\[
\Delta' = \frac{r}{R_0} \left[ \beta_p(r) + \frac{l_i(r)}{2} \right]
\]
with: \[
\beta_p(r) = -2 \frac{R_0^2 q^2}{B_0^2 r^4} \int_0^r \hat{r}^2 P' d\hat{r}, \quad l_i(r) = \frac{2q^2}{r^4} \int_0^r d\hat{r} \frac{\hat{r}^3}{q^2}
\]
Explain that \(\Delta(r)\) describes the compression of the poloidal magnetic flux in the \(\nabla R\) direction, and describe briefly the two forces related to \(\beta_p\) and \(l_i\) that cause this compression.



For a shearless plasma, for which \(q\) is constant, write down the simple relation for \(\psi''/\psi'\), substitute this into Eq. (5) and solve the resulting differential equation for \(S_m(r)\) assuming boundary conditions \(S_m(0) = 0\), and \(S_m(r=a) = S_m(a)\) (constants), where \(a\) is the edge of the plasma.
Comment on the radial dependence (from the plasma edge going inwards) of elongation \(\kappa(r)\) and triangularity \(\delta(r)\), where these are respectively:
\[
\kappa = \frac{r - S_2}{r + S_2}, \quad \delta = \frac{4S_3}{r}
\]
How would positive shear affect this penetration?

title: Exercise 2: Grad-Shafranov Expansion and Shaping
text:
The lecture notes adopt the coordinate system \((r, \omega, \phi)\) for an inverse aspect ratio expansion and Fourier representation of the cylindrical coordinates \((R, Z, \phi)\):
\[

\[

\]

\[

\]
with: \[
\beta_p(r) = -2 \frac{R_0^2 q^2}{B_0^2 r^4} \int_0^r \hat{r}^2 P' d\hat{r}, \quad l_i(r) = \frac{2q^2}{r^4} \int_0^r d\hat{r} \frac{\hat{r}^3}{q^2}
\]

For a shearless plasma, for which \(q\) is constant, write down the simple relation for \(\psi''/\psi'\), substitute this into Eq. (5) and solve the resulting differential equation for \(S_m(r)\) assuming boundary conditions \(S_m(0) = 0\), and \(S_m(r=a) = S_m(a)\) (constants), where \(a\) is the edge of the plasma.

\[

title: Exercise 2: Grad-Shafranov Expansion and Shaping
text:
Solutions

Solution (a): Magnetic Well
1. Expansion of Terms We start with the equilibrium equation for the \(m=0\) mode (Eq. 3): \[\frac{1}{2r^2} (r^2 \psi'^2)' + R_0^2 P' + FF' = 0\]
Using the definition \(\psi' \approx r B_0 / q\) (leading order approximation where \(F \approx R_0 B_0\)), we expand the first term: \[\psi'^2 \approx \frac{r^2 B_0^2}{q^2}\] \[\frac{1}{2r^2} (r^2 \psi'^2)' = \frac{B_0^2}{2r^2} \frac{d}{dr} \left( \frac{r^4}{q^2} \right)\] Using the definition of magnetic shear \(s = (r/q) q'\), the derivative becomes: \[\frac{d}{dr} \left( \frac{r^4}{q^2} \right) = \frac{4r^3 q^2 - 2r^4 q q'}{q^4} = \frac{r^3}{q^2} (4 - 2s)\] Thus, the first term is: \[\text{Term 1} = \frac{B_0^2 r}{q^2} (2 - s)\]
Next, we expand the \(FF'\) term using \(F = R_0 B_0 (1 + \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} F_2)\): \[FF' = \frac{1}{2} (F^2)' \approx \frac{1}{2} (R_0^2 B_0^2 (1 + 2\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} F_2))' = R_0^2 B_0^2 \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} F_2'\]
2. Solving for \(F_2\) Substituting these back into Eq. (3): \[\frac{B_0^2 r}{q^2} (2 - s) + R_0^2 P' + R_0^2 B_0^2 \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} F_2' = 0\]
Isolating \(F_2'\): \[F_2' = - \frac{P'}{\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} B_0^2} - \frac{r (2-s)}{R_0^2 \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} q^2}\]
Integrating from \(0\) to \(r\): \[F_2(r) = - \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^{-2}} \frac{P(r)}{B_0^2} - \textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^{-2}} \int_0^r \frac{r' dr'}{R_0^2} \left( \frac{2-s(r')}{q(r')^2} \right)\] This matches Eq. (6).
3. Physical Explanation The magnetic field strength is dominated by the toroidal field \(B_\phi \propto F(r)\). In a vacuum, \(F\) is constant. In a plasma, \(F\) is modified by \(\textcolor{red}{\varepsilon^2} F_2\). Both terms in \(F_2\) are typically negative: 1. Pressure term: \(-P/B_0^2\) is negative because plasma is diamagnetic (resisting the external field). 2. Shear term: The integral of \((2-s)\) is generally positive (for moderate shear), making the term negative.
Since \(F_{plasma} < F_{vacuum}\), the magnetic field strength is reduced in the center, creating a magnetic well. This configuration is favorable for stability.



Solution (b): Shafranov Shift
1. Integration We rewrite Eq. (4) by multiplying by an integrating factor \(r(\psi')^2\): \[\frac{d}{dr} \left( r(\psi')^2 \Delta' \right) = \frac{r(\psi')^2}{R_0} - 2r^2 R_0 P'\]
Integrating from \(0\) to \(r\): \[r(\psi')^2 \Delta' = \frac{1}{R_0} \int_0^r \hat{r} (\psi')^2 d\hat{r} - 2R_0 \int_0^r \hat{r}^2 P' d\hat{r}\]
2. Substitution Using the approximation \(\psi' \approx r B_0 / q\): \[\Delta' = \frac{1}{r (r^2 B_0^2 / q^2) R_0} \int_0^r \hat{r} \frac{\hat{r}^2 B_0^2}{q^2} d\hat{r} - \frac{2R_0}{r (r^2 B_0^2 / q^2)} \int_0^r \hat{r}^2 P' d\hat{r}\]
Simplifying the terms: * Term 1: \(\frac{q^2}{r^3 R_0} \int_0^r \frac{\hat{r}^3}{q^2} d\hat{r} = \frac{r}{R_0} \left[ \frac{q^2}{r^4} \int \frac{\hat{r}^3}{q^2} d\hat{r} \right] = \frac{r}{R_0} \frac{l_i(r)}{2}\) * Term 2: \(- \frac{2 R_0 q^2}{r^3 B_0^2} \int_0^r \hat{r}^2 P' d\hat{r} = \frac{r}{R_0} \left[ - \frac{2 R_0^2 q^2}{B_0^2 r^4} \int \hat{r}^2 P' d\hat{r} \right] = \frac{r}{R_0} \beta_p(r)\)
Resulting in: \[\Delta' = \frac{r}{R_0} \left( \beta_p(r) + \frac{l_i(r)}{2} \right)\]
3. Physical Explanation \(\Delta(r)\) represents the Shafranov Shift, an outward displacement of magnetic flux surfaces. This shift compresses flux surfaces on the outboard (\(\nabla R\)) side. The two forces driving this are: 1. \(\beta_p\) (Kinetic Pressure): The “tire-tube” force where plasma pressure tries to expand the torus volume. 2. \(l_i\) (Internal Inductance): The “hoop force” where the current loop tries to expand radially due to self-repulsion.



Solution (c): Shearless Plasma
1. Differential Equation For shearless plasma (\(q = \text{const}\)), \(\psi' \propto r\), so \(\psi''/\psi' = 1/r\). Substituting into Eq. (5): \[S_m'' + \frac{3}{r} S_m' + \frac{1-m^2}{r^2} S_m = 0\] Using the trial solution \(S_m \sim r^k\), we solve the characteristic equation \(k^2 + 2k + (1-m^2) = 0\), yielding roots \(k = -1 \pm m\). Discarding the divergent root (\(r^{-(m+1)}\)), we get: \[S_m(r) = S_m(a) \left( \frac{r}{a} \right)^{m-1}\]
2. Shaping Penetration * Elongation (\(m=2\)): \(S_2 \propto r\). \[\kappa(r) = \frac{r - Cr}{r + Cr} = \text{constant}\] Elongation is constant from edge to core. * Triangularity (\(m=3\)): \(S_3 \propto r^2\). \[\delta(r) = \frac{4 C r^2}{r} \propto r\] Triangularity vanishes linearly towards the core.
3. Effect of Positive Shear With positive shear (\(s > 0\)), the damping term in the differential equation decreases (\(\frac{3-2s}{r}\)). This leads to a larger characteristic exponent \(k\). A larger \(k\) means faster radial decay as \(r \to 0\). Thus, positive shear inhibits the penetration of shaping into the core; the core becomes circular faster than in the shearless case.

title: Exercise 7: Trapped Particles and Fast Ion Stability
text:
For an isotropic distribution of fast ions \(F_h(\mathcal{E}, r)\):

(a) Show that the fraction of trapped ions is exactly (the result holding in any magnetic configuration, e.g. 3D though in thin banana limit): \[
    f_t = \left( 1 - \frac{B}{B_{max}} \right)^{1/2}
    \] and describe the meaning of \(B_{max}\).
(b) Assume now a large aspect ratio axisymmetric tokamak (or reverse field pinch), show that the local trapped fraction is approximately, \[
    f_t = \epsilon^{1/2} (1 + \cos \Theta)^{1/2}
    \] and discuss the trapped fraction on the outboard side (low field side), the inboard side (high field side) and calculate the approximate flux surface average value, \[
    \overline{f_t} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} d\Theta f_t.
    \]
(c) The solution to the drift kinetic equation for the perturbed distribution function in the limit of large bounce and transit frequency is quoted in the lecture notes to be approximately, \[
    \delta F_h = - \boldsymbol{\xi} \cdot \nabla F_h + \begin{cases} q_j \langle \phi \rangle \xi^\psi \frac{\partial F_h}{\partial \mathcal{E}} \frac{(\omega - n\omega_{*h})}{\omega - n\langle \dot{\phi} \rangle + i \nu_{eff}} & \text{trapped ions} \\ 0 & \text{passing ions}, \end{cases}
    \] where \[
    \omega_{*h} = q_j^{-1} \frac{\partial F_h}{\partial \psi} \bigg/ \frac{\partial F_h}{\partial \mathcal{E}}.
    \] Show in the collisional limit that \[
    \frac{\delta P_{\parallel h} + \delta P_{\perp h}}{2} = - \boldsymbol{\xi} \cdot \nabla P_h = - \xi^r \frac{dP_h}{dr}
    \] and in the collisionless low frequency limit (\(\omega \to 0\)), \[
    \frac{\delta P_{\parallel h} + \delta P_{\perp h}}{2} = - \xi^r \frac{dP_h}{dr} + \frac{m_h}{2} \xi^r \int_{trap} d^3v \left( v_{\parallel}^2 + \frac{v_{\perp}^2}{2} \right) \frac{\partial F_h}{\partial r} \tag{19}
    \] where the integral is over trapped velocity space.
(d) Assuming strong fast ion density gradients, it is shown in the lecture notes that the potential energy associated with the second term in Eq. (19) is approximately, \[
    \delta W_k = 2\pi^2 \int_0^a dr \ r (\xi^r)^2 \left( - R_0 \frac{dP_h}{dr} \right) \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) \overline{(- f_t (\boldsymbol{\kappa} \cdot \nabla r))}.
    \] Describe the physical significance of \((-dP_h/dr) \overline{(-f_t \boldsymbol{\kappa} \cdot \nabla r)}\) in words and by calculating it for lowest order approximation for the curvature \(\boldsymbol{\kappa}\). Show that \[
    \delta W_k = \frac{2\pi^2 B_0^2}{R_0} \int_0^a dr \frac{(\xi^r)^2}{q^2} \frac{\alpha_h \overline{f_t}}{4}
    \] where \[
    \alpha_h = - \frac{2q^2 R_0}{B_0^2} \frac{dP_h}{dr}.
    \]
(e) Adding the previous result to the interchange contributions to \(\delta W\) in an axisymmetric torus we have approximately, \[
    \delta W = \frac{2\pi^2 B_0^2}{R_0} \int_0^a dr \ r \frac{(\xi^r)^2}{q^2} \left[ \alpha \epsilon \left( 1 - \frac{1}{q_s^2} \right) + \frac{\alpha_h \overline{f_t}}{4} \right],
    \] where \(\alpha\) involves the full pressure \(P\) (electron, thermal ion and hot ion), \[
    \alpha = - \frac{2q^2 R_0}{B_0^2} \frac{dP}{dr}.
    \] Show in terms of an ordering in \(\epsilon\) how large \(\alpha_h\) must be relative to \(\alpha\) so that the fast particles have an important impact on stability (you may assume \(1/q_s^2 - 1 \sim 1\)).
